title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an artist called Sophie talking about a community ceramics studio. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an artist called Sophie talking about a community ceramics studio. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: An artist called Sophie talks about a community ceramics studio.
extracts.audio_script: Good afternoon. I’m Sophie and I run Clay Corner, a ceramics studio where local people can learn how to make useful and decorative items from clay. The project started in my home workshop with space for only four students. When demand increased, the council helped us find a larger building. We now operate from a former [9]bus garage[/9], which does not sound artistic, but its wide entrance, strong floor and high ceiling are ideal for moving materials and working safely. The interior has been made warm and welcoming.||Adults and teenagers can join our beginner courses, and there is no need to have studied art previously. Many people arrive hoping to make complex vases straight away. We begin with an object that is simpler but still attractive: in their first practical session, students produce small [10]bowls[/10], learning to shape the clay evenly by hand. Before that, the initial demonstration focuses on [11]clay preparation[/11], since air inside the material can lead to cracks when items are heated. Students practise this carefully before trying to form anything of their own.||We supply clay, tools and protective clothing, so students do not need to buy special equipment. However, I ask them to bring a [12]sketchbook[/12], where they can develop design ideas and make notes about colours or shapes they would like to try. Completed pieces cannot simply dry and be taken home. They must be fired in our large [13]electric kiln[/13], which reaches extremely high temperatures and is operated only by trained staff. Students are told when to return and collect their work once it is safe. This process often takes longer than beginners expect.||As students become confident, they can attend one-off events. Last year's workshop on making plant pots was extremely popular. This term, the first special class to fill was the [14]glazing workshop[/14], where learners discover how surface colours change after firing. We also contribute to local spaces. A café near the station asked us to make decorative [15]wall tiles[/15] for its entrance, and students on the advanced course are designing these together. They will include shapes inspired by local buildings rather than individual names. It is a real commission and an exciting opportunity.||Our learners like sharing their work. At the end of the summer, we will have a table at the town [16]craft market[/16], where students may display or sell items if they wish. Anyone planning to apply to an art college can receive help photographing work for a [17]portfolio[/17], and former students say this has been very useful. Finally, our studio would like to involve people who cannot attend regular courses. We are raising funds for a large outdoor [18]community mural[/18] made of ceramic pieces created by residents at free weekend sessions. I have course dates and application forms here, and will be happy to discuss which class is best for you.

dialogue.speaker: sophie
dialogue.text: Good afternoon. I’m Sophie and I run Clay Corner, a ceramics studio where local people can learn how to make useful and decorative items from clay. The project started in my home workshop with space for only four students. When demand increased, the council helped us find a larger building. We now operate from a former bus garage, which does not sound artistic, but its wide entrance, strong floor and high ceiling are ideal for moving materials and working safely. The interior has been made warm and welcoming.||Adults and teenagers can join our beginner courses, and there is no need to have studied art previously. Many people arrive hoping to make complex vases straight away. We begin with an object that is simpler but still attractive: in their first practical session, students produce small bowls, learning to shape the clay evenly by hand. Before that, the initial demonstration focuses on clay preparation, since air inside the material can lead to cracks when items are heated. Students practise this carefully before trying to form anything of their own.||We supply clay, tools and protective clothing, so students do not need to buy special equipment. However, I ask them to bring a sketchbook, where they can develop design ideas and make notes about colours or shapes they would like to try. Completed pieces cannot simply dry and be taken home. They must be fired in our large electric kiln, which reaches extremely high temperatures and is operated only by trained staff. Students are told when to return and collect their work once it is safe. This process often takes longer than beginners expect.||As students become confident, they can attend one-off events. Last year's workshop on making plant pots was extremely popular. This term, the first special class to fill was the glazing workshop, where learners discover how surface colours change after firing. We also contribute to local spaces. A café near the station asked us to make decorative wall tiles for its entrance, and students on the advanced course are designing these together. They will include shapes inspired by local buildings rather than individual names. It is a real commission and an exciting opportunity.||Our learners like sharing their work. At the end of the summer, we will have a table at the town craft market, where students may display or sell items if they wish. Anyone planning to apply to an art college can receive help photographing work for a portfolio, and former students say this has been very useful. Finally, our studio would like to involve people who cannot attend regular courses. We are raising funds for a large outdoor community mural made of ceramic pieces created by residents at free weekend sessions. I have course dates and application forms here, and will be happy to discuss which class is best for you.
